entries.name: Harvesting
entries: The Grey Knight's arms can be each preserved with a component DC of 15.

The bonemonger has the following unique components, in addition to ones normal for an aberration. The food component for the unique recipe is dire rabbit flesh, which is used to craft braised berry bunny (see page 16).

entries.caption: Bonemonger Harvest Table
footnotes: E Edible component

entries.name: Crafting
entries: The following unique items can be crafted from the bonemonger's and Grey Knight's components.

entries.caption: Bonemonger Craftable Items
footnotes: * This is the 'off the shelf' purchase cost and can vary significantly from the crafting cost.

entries.name: Cooking
entries: Using dire rabbit flesh, as well as blood, bone, and brain, you can make a {@dc 24} Constitution (cook's utensils) check to craft a {@i braised berry bunny} magical meal. See pages 137-149 in Heliana's Guide to Monster Hunting for rules on cooking.

entries.name: Braised Berry Bunny
entries: You gain a number of charges that increases based on the meal's rarity. At any time while you are under the influence of this magical meal's effects, you can spend 1 charge to imbue your words with the power of veracity when you ask a creature a question. The creature must succeed on a Charisma saving throw or be unable to speak a deliberate lie for the next minute. The DC of the saving throw increases with rarity.

You know whether the creature succeeds or fails on its saving throw. An affected creature is aware of the spell and can thus avoid answering questions to which it would normally respond with a lie. Such creatures can be evasive in its answers as long as it remains within the boundaries of the truth.

book.name: L'Arsene's Ledger of Treasures and Trinkets
book.author: Loot Tavern

data.name: Chapter 1: Introduction

entries: Tax. Free. You heard it; we're outwith the jurisdiction of any council, trade conglomerate, nation, or empire. We're free of the tyranny of governmental bureaucracy, unsoiled by boring bookkeeping, and unburdened by the volatility of trade unions. We also don't have free healthcare, any education system, and are frequently raided by space pirates, but they never last long.
So, browse my ledger or commission a unique item, safe in the knowledge that the price will not change at the register. Unless I feel like it, of course.

Welcome to L'Arsene's Ledger of Treasures and Trinkets (LLTT), a one-stop shop for 5th edition magical accoutrement. Within these pages you'll find over 700 magic items, a guide for how to design your own magic items, as well as several bar-themed subclasses and familiars.

entries.name: Structure
entries: This book has three parts:

items.name: Part One:
entries: Equipment, magic items, and the magic item crafting workshop.

items.name: Part Two:
entries: Subclasses and familiars.

items.name: Part Three:
entries: The Appendices; spells (Appendix A) and monsters (Appendix B).
There's an index at the end of the book, as well as links to our website and Patreon where you can acquire even more items.

entries.name: Formatting & Features
entries: This book introduces new formatting and interactivity to enhance your gaming experience.

entries.name: Formatting
entries: This book uses {@b bolding}, {@i italics}, and {@color colour|372dcc} to help key information be identified at a glance.

items.name: Bolding
entries: Monster names ("a {@creature roper"}), dice calculations ("{@dice 1d6 + 3} fire damage"), check modifiers ("{@hit 7} to hit"), conditions that are being applied ("fall {@condition prone}"), areas & distances ("a 30-foot cone"), and {@b advantage} & {@b disadvantage} are bolded.

items.name: Italics
entries: Spell names ("{@spell purple rain}"), magic items ("{@item lichslayer|LALoTT}"), foreign languages ("speak mellon and enter"), and book & boat names ("{@i L'Arsene's Ledger of Treasure and Trinkets}") are italicised.

items.name: Colour
entries: Checks and saving throws use a coloured bold format. Saving throws are always fully bolded ("{@dc 15} Dexterity saving throw"), while only the DC, ability, and skill is bolded for checks ("{@dc 15} Strength (Persuasion) check").

entries.name: Language
entries: The content herein is written in British English. Prepare yourselves for the colourful armour of paralysing, for referring to 'the party' as interchangeably 3rd-person singular ("the party has been killed") and plural ("the party are in trouble"), and archaic language like "whilst" & "amongst". Where items and creatures have established names, like armor of invulnerability or a {@creature gray ooze}, those remain unchanged.

entries.name: Non-standard Ability Checks
entries: {@i L'Arsene's Ledger} follows {@i Heliana's Guide} in breaking from the standard association of abilities and skills, allowing you to be creative in how you mix and match them. For example, {@skill Persuasion} is usually a Charisma check. However, if a character states that they break a femur with their bare hands to scare a bandit, you might ask for a Strength ({@skill Intimidation}) check. All this requires the player to do is make a Strength check and, if they have proficiency in {@skill Intimidation}, add their proficiency bonus to the result. Tool proficiencies are referenced just like skill proficiencies: a Strength ({@item smith's tools|PHB}) check means that you must make a Strength check and, if you have proficiency in {@item smith's tools|PHB}, add your proficiency bonus to the result. If something, perhaps a magic item, says that you gain "advantage on {@skill Stealth} checks", this means that you gain advantage on all checks that use the {@skill Stealth} skill, regardless of which ability is used. When checks use the word 'or', this indicates either skill/tool or ability can be used in any combination. For example, a Strength or Dexterity ({@skill Athletics} or {@item woodcarver's tools|PHB}) check means you can use any one of the four check permutations: Strength ({@skill Athletics}), Dexterity ({@skill Athletics}), Strength ({@item woodcarver's tools|PHB}), or Dexterity ({@item woodcarver's tools|PHB}).

entries.name: Rule Zero
entries: As always, if you're all having fun, you're doing it right. Take the pieces of this book that you love, tweak the ones you like, and ignore the ones that suck. Make this book your own and use it to create unforgettable memories with your friends. Then tell us about it on our Discord server ({@link heliana.lt/Discord|heliana.lt/Discord}) so we know the blood, sweat, and tears were worth it!

entries.name: Drinking & Drunkenness
entries: Drinking is a balancing act: too much and you become inebriated; just enough and you become a master at billiards, charming as a devil, and capable of surmounting any obstacle. Legend says the Pale Aleomancers, led by famous brewer Jan Smyth, titrated themselves to reach the perfect level of drunkenness. In the short time thereafter, conflicts between long-warring nations were resolved, a universal healthcare system was established that delivered free care at the point of need, and the eternal question of why the gobboc crossed the road was answered. To celebrate their achievement, they finished their pint. In the slightly-more-inebriated aftermath, all their hard work was undone and the world descended into chaos once more. Since then, Aleomancers the world over have kept their titrating techniques a closely guarded secret.

data.name: Chapter 2: Equipment

entries: Sure, Heliana loves a battleaxe, Humperdink's fond of a glaive, and that thunder god, Thorr, has a special and intimate relationship with that hammer of his, but that's all a bit boring isn't it? You've got to think outside the box; that's why I call myself an outventer, not an inventer. Check out my menagerie of melee machines and foolproof firearms. You break it, you buy it. It breaks you, you buy it. It unexpectedly explodes when you first use it, you buy it.

This chapter presents several new weapon types, as well as those previously introduced in {@book Heliana's Guide|HelianasGuidetoMonsterHunting}. You'll find a breakdown of the new properties presented in these items, followed by a summary of each new weapon type. The chapter culminates with a table of the new weapons, including their typical prices and the tools with which they can be crafted.

entries.name: New Properties
entries: Some weapons in this chapter use new properties, listed below. In addition, one property, Reload, has been modified.

entries.name: Magitech Firearms
entries: Magitech firearms (martial ranged weapons) use a small arcanomagnetic engine to propel their ferrous projectiles at incredible velocities. Though their means of propulsion is magical, the damage they deal remains nonmagical. Such firearms are always loud and tend to have poor accuracy over long ranges, although the recently-introduced rifling found in revolvers and rifles increases their effective range.

entries.name: Extra Reloads
entries: More expensive models of magitech firearms have a self-reloading mechanism that harnesses the arcanomagnetic engine to re-cock the weapon. If you wish, you can increase the magazine capacity of a magitech firearm up to a maximum of 6, ensuring that the price increases proportionally. For example, a pistol typically costs 200 gp and has reload (2). If you increased this to reload (6), you would multiply the price by 3 for a new cost of 600 gp.

entries.name: Types
entries: There are several types of magitech firearm currently found in the world.

data.name: Chapter 3: Magical Metadata

entries: Sure, you can try to craft everything yourself; I'll happily sell you the rarest and most expensive ingredients, then watch you waste them in an abysmal enchanting attempt. Or you can employ me and my bona fide forging prowess and save us all from your handiwork.

This chapter acts like a guidebook for the 700 items in the next chapter. We'll first cover the stripped-down harvesting and crafting rules from Heliana's Guide to Monster Hunting, giving you what you need to create the items found in this tome. This is followed by tables summarising every magic item in the book, including the crafting recipes to make those items. Lastly, we present rollable loot tables, analogous to those in the core 5e rules, to help you discover what's in the dragon's hoard.

entries.name: Harvesting Lite
entries: This section details the core concepts of Harvesting, allowing you to introduce the system to your table. To furnish your game with a suite of additional options, refer to Chapter 4 in {@book Heliana's Guide to Monster Hunting|HelianasGuidetoMonsterHunting|3}. There you'll find additional rules like volatile components that can explode, ruining components with destructive damage types, salvaging old magic items, ritual carving using spellcasting abilities instead of Dexterity, finding traders to buy and sell components, and a pricing system that complements the magic item prices in this book. A heaping of worked examples helps to make sense of all these new mechanics, and there's a whole section on mundane ingredients and crafting magic items directly from the bones and claws of fallen foes: monster-forged items!

entries.name: Cooking
entries: In addition, all of the rules for cooking magical meals, a new type of magic item, are described in {@book Heliana's Guide|HelianasGuidetoMonsterHunting|4|Cooking}. For the sake of plenitude, components in the harvesting tables that are edible are marked using a superscript 'E' ({@sup E}).

entries.name: Harvesting Rules
entries: After a creature is slain, its components must be harvested quickly before their magic fades. There are five steps involved in this process.

entries.name: Step 1 - Description
entries: When a creature dies, the GM consults the harvest table corresponding to the creature's type and determines which of the listed components are available to be harvested.

entries.name: Example
entries: In the example below, the party wishes to harvest an aboleth they have slain. The GM consults the {@table Aberration Harvest table|LALoTT} and decides that the aberration has the following components on offer: 3 {@i eyes}, 5 {@i tentacles}, 50 {@i phials of mucus}, 50 {@i phials of blood}, a {@i pouch of teeth}, its {@i brain}, and its {@i hide}. Because an aboleth is CR 10, one can also harvest {@i robust essence} from it.

entries.name: GM:
entries: In addition to the many phials' worth of blood and mucus, you can harvest three eyes, five tentacles, a pouch-worth of teeth, its brain, and its rubbery hide. Because this creature is particularly potent, you can also try to extract its essence—a difficult process.

entries.name: Step 2 - Harvest List
entries: The harvesters then quickly decide what they want to harvest and in which order. This order is known as the harvest list.

entries.name: Example
entries: The party chooses to harvest the following components in the listed order: a pouch of teeth, 3 eyes, the hide, and then the essence.

entries.name: Gurf:
entries: I want the eyeses. And all the teef!

entries.name: Mizzard:
entries: Very well, Gurf, but let's not forget the hide we came here for! And we'll need essence to be able to make the more powerful 'rare' versions of items. We'll take a pouch of teeth, the three eyes, the hide, and then the essence, in that order.

entries.name: Step 3 - Harvest DCs
entries: After the party has created its harvest list, the GM calculates the Harvest DCs. They list out the chosen components in the order the party wishes to harvest them and sequentially add each Component DC to the total of all the previous Component DCs. The Component DC represents how hard a component is to harvest.

entries.name: Step 4 - Harvesting check
entries: The players make a Harvesting check. A Harvesting check is the combined total of two ability checks: an Assessment check and a Carving check; these are described in more detail on page 13.

entries.name: GM:
entries: Okay. Mizzard, as the assessing harvester, you need to make an Intelligence ({@skill Arcana}) check and Gurf, as the carving harvester, you need to make a Dexterity ({@skill Arcana}) check.

entries.name: Mizzard:
entries: Okay... that's a 12 plus 5. 17!

entries.name: Gurf:
entries: What's Dexterity ({@skill Arcana})? It's not on my sheet.

entries.name: GM:
entries: Make a Dexterity check—roll a d20 and add your Dexterity modifier—then add your proficiency bonus if you're proficient in {@skill Arcana}.

entries.name: Gurf:
entries: Okay that's a... 16. My Dex gives me plus 4, but I'm not proficient. 20!

entries.name: GM:
entries: Great; 17 plus 20—that's a total of 37.

entries.name: Step 5 - Loot
entries: Compare the result of the Harvesting check to the harvest list you made in step 3. If the Harvesting check's result met or exceeded the Harvest DC for a component, that component is successfully harvested.

entries.name: Example
entries: A result of 37 means that everything except the hide and essence are acquired.

entries.name: GM:
entries: The three eyes come out, plop plop plop, as do the teeth. Unfortunately, the hide is too tough and you can't harvest it before its magic seeps away. You never even get started on the essence.

entries.name: Gurf:
entries: Yay! Teef and eyez!

entries.name: Mizzard:
entries: Blast—I wanted that hide...

entries.name: Components & Difficulty
entries: Harvesting terms and concepts are explained below.

entries.name: Component DC
entries: Some components, like {@i teeth}, are very simple to extricate, whilst others, like {@i hide}, take a great deal of skill to extract without ruining them. Each component has a Component DC, indicating how hard it is to safely extract before it loses its magical potency. The tables describe the different components that varying creature types might yield.

entries.name: Quantity
entries: The GM decides how many of each component are available for harvest based on the creature. For example, a severely damaged aboleth might only yield 1 {@i eye} and 1 {@i tentacle}, rather than its usual bounty.

entries.name: Harvest List
entries: Components can be harvested in any order, regardless of Component DC. The harvest list details which components the harvesters wish to attempt to extract and in which order. The harvesters must agree on the harvest list together or defer to the carving harvester.

entries.name: Harvest DC
entries: After the harvesters establish their harvest list, calculate the Harvest DCs. This is achieved by adding the Component DC of each component in the list to the DC of the components higher up in the list. These cumulative DCs are known as the Harvest DCs; harvesters successfully extract the components for which they meet or exceed a Harvest DC.

entries.name: Duration & Degradation
entries: Harvesting a creature takes a set duration based on the size of the creature (see harvest time in the table below).

entries.name: Degradation
entries: To keep the game flowing, these rules offer the concept of degradation; the adventurers have only a short time, post-mortem, to harvest components. Spells like {@spell gentle repose} do not prevent this. This rule prevents the aftermath of each fight from becoming a harvest-fest by limiting the number of creatures that can be harvested.

entries.name: Time Limit
entries: For harvesting to yield magical components, a harvester must begin harvesting a corpse within 1 minute of the creature's death and, once it has begun harvesting, not cease harvesting the corpse for the duration of the check. As the shortest harvest time of any creature is 5 minutes, a character only has time to harvest one creature after a battle before the other creatures have degraded.

entries.name: Assessment and Carving
entries: The Harvesting check is the summed total of two ability checks: Assessment and Carving. A single creature can choose to make both checks; if it does so, it makes these checks with {@b disadvantage}. With all Harvesting checks, the skill used for the check depends on the type of creature the characters are attempting to harvest. For example, beasts require a {@skill Survival} check while aberrations require an {@skill Arcana} check.

entries.name: Assessment
entries: To correctly assess how best to extract and store creature components, a character must make an Intelligence check. The skill applicable to the check depends on the type of creature, as shown in the {@table Creature Types and Associated Skills|LALoTT|Creature Types and Associated Skills table}. A creature attempting this Assessment check is known as the assessing harvester.

entries.name: Carving
entries: Skill with a knife is the proven method of harvesting components. A creature attempting to harvest a corpse makes a Dexterity check. The skill applicable to the check depends on the type of creature, as shown in the {@table Creature Types and Associated Skills|LALoTT|Creature Types and Associated Skills table}. A creature attempting this Carving check is known as the carving harvester.

entries.name: Harvesting check
entries.text: Assessment check result + Carving check result

entries.name: Spells and Buffs
entries: For a spell or magical effect to have any influence on the outcome of harvesting, it must affect a harvester for the entire duration of the Harvesting check (see Creature Size and Harvest Time table on page 13). For this reason, spells with a duration of 1 minute, like {@spell bless} and {@spell guidance}, never confer their bonus to the result of the check. A spell like {@spell enhance ability}, which lasts 1 hour, could confer its advantage to a Harvesting check so long as the spell begins before the check starts and does not end until after the check is completed.

entries.name: Helpers
entries: Creatures not involved in assessment or carving can help! The number of creatures that can help depends on the size of the creature being harvested (see table below). If a helper has proficiency in the skill associated with the monster's type, the helper adds its proficiency bonus to the Harvesting check's result. If the helper doesn't have this proficiency, it adds half its proficiency bonus rounded down, instead. Helpers must help for the entire duration of the harvesting procedure to add this bonus. This takes the place of the Help action, which has no effect in Harvesting or Crafting.

entries.name: Results & Rewards
entries: A character receives each component for which it meets or exceeds the DC in the harvest list. This is cumulative; for example, a Harvesting check of 37 on the aboleth example on page 12 yields three {@i eyes} and a {@i pouch of teeth}. The {@i hide} and {@i robust essence} are not harvested, as the result of 37 is lower than the DCs (45 and 75, respectively).

entries.name: Component Types
entries: Component names are comprised of the creature type (e.g. beast) and the creature part (e.g. horn). A rhino, demon, and minotaur all have {@i horns}, but these are known as {@i beast horn}, {@i fiend horn}, and {@i monstrosity horn}, respectively, and are used to craft different things.

entries.name: Optional Rule: Metatags
entries: Metatags are a tool that expands the number of possible components in the game, increasing the specificity of the crafting system.

entries.name: Components
entries: When using metatags, you record the name of each component you harvest. For example, without metatags, a horn from a rhinoceros and a {@i horn} from a goat are both known as {@i beast horns}. With metatags, these are two different horns: a {@i beast (rhinoceros) horn} and a {@i beast (goat) horn}.

entries.name: Recipes
entries: In crafting recipes, metatags specify a detail about the creature that bore the component written in parentheses. This detail could be all or part of its name (e.g. Animated), size (e.g. Large), subcategory (e.g. shapechanger), or something else. You may have to look at the creature's statistics to see if it satisfies a metatag requirement.

entries.name: Quests & Consequences
entries: Metatags can be used in either or both of the following ways. Firstly, a GM may require that a specific metatag is needed to craft an item. For example, a GM may decide that {@item flame tongue}—a fiery weapon—might be craftable only using the {@i breath sac} from a fire-breathing (brass, gold, or red) dragon. A player wouldn't be able to use a {@i dragon (ancient white dragon) breath sac} to craft the item and would need to embark on a quest to find the appropriate component.

Secondly, if the correct metatag is used in a crafting recipe, a GM can reward the player by granting them {@b advantage} on the check to craft the item. For example, without metatags, any {@i dragon's eye} can be used to craft a {@item ring of poison resistance}. With metatags, an adventurer can craft the ring with the eye of any {@i dragon}, but has {@b advantage} on the check(s) if they use the {@i eye} of a green dragon.

entries.name: Harvest Tables
entries: This section presents the components that may be available for each creature type. When a player declares their intention to harvest a creature, consult the relevant table in this section and choose the components that make sense for the creature. For example, an aboleth has tentacles, eyes, mucus, blood, and a hide, but doesn't have claws, chitin, or antennae. Boss monster can have unique components that aren't found in these harvest tables.

entries.name: Usability
entries: Not all the components in the harvest tables presented herein have an attached recipe in this book,{@i  L'Arsene's Ledger}. However, between {@i Heliana's Guide} and {@i L'Arsene's Ledger}, every harvestable component can be used to make something. You can scan or click the QR code opposite to visit the Loot Tavern Vault, a webtool that, when complete, lets you enter the monster components you have available and generate a list of items you can craft.

entries.name: Superscripts
entries: Some harvestable components have small superscript letters after their name. A '{@sup v}' indicates the component is volatile (optional rule in Heliana's Guide), an 'E' indicates they are edible (but aren't used to cr{@i }aft items), while an 'E+' indicates they are both edible and used in crafting. Components whose edible category is not obvious (such as {@i primordial dust} being a spice) are indicated with an asterisk and explained below the table.

entries.name: Unusual Anatomy
entries: If a creature has some interesting anatomy not on the appropriate harvest table, you can grab the component and DC from a different creature type's harvest table.

entries.name: Essence
entries: {@i Essence} is required to craft more powerful magic items. It comes in five forms: frail, robust, potent, mythic, and deific. These {@i essences} are required to craft items of uncommon, rare, very rare, legendary, and artifact rarity, respectively. Whilst {@i essence} can be extracted from all creature types, the essence available depends on the creature's Challenge Rating (CR). The following harvest table can be appended to all the monster harvest tables in this section.
You can't harvest a lower-level {@i essence} from a higher CR creature—they have only one {@i essence}, the one dictated by its CR!

entries.name: Appearance
entries: Essence can look like whatever you want it to: a nebulous ball of energy, a random creature component, or something you extract into a crystal to make it glow. This is intentionally undefined to let you build your own world!

entries.name: Essence & Balance
entries: Because essence can only be gained by harvesting high CR creatures, it acts as a gating mechanism to limit the power of items the party can craft. If you want an especially high magic campaign, consider decreasing the CR at which essence can be harvested. This will allow more powerful items to be crafted at lower levels. If you want a low-magic campaign, do the opposite.

entries.name: GM Tip: Boss Essence
entries: When it comes to crafting, only an essence's rarity matters, not the creature it comes from. The most valuable parts of boss monsters are their unique components, not their essence!

entries.name: Crafting Lite
entries: The crafting rules below provide what you need to know to craft each item in this book. To make your harvesting and crafting journey even richer, refer to {@book Chapters 4|HelianasGuidetoMonsterHunting|3} and {@book 5|HelianasGuidetoMonsterHunting|4} in {@book Heliana's Guide to Monster Hunting|HelianasGuidetoMonsterHunting}. There you'll find exciting concepts like quirks (random boons and flaws when you exceed or don't meet the check's DC), hiring craftspeople, working in crafting teams, feats for becoming an expert or master crafter, a whole heap of optional rules, as well as rules for salvaging, socketing, and upgrading items.
You can't enchant a sunsplitter spear without first creating a {@item spear|PHB}. Within this guide, there are two types of crafting, each associated with a type of crafting check:

items: {@b Manufacturing} is the creation of mundane (nonmagical) items from raw materials by a creature proficient with a tool using a Manufacturing check.
{@b Enchanting} is the magification of a mundane item by a spellcaster to give it magical powers using an Enchanting check.

entries: {@variantrule Crafting|HelianasGuidetoMonsterHunting|Forging}, detailed in {@book Heliana's Guide|HelianasGuidetoMonsterHunting|}, is where both these processes are combined, allowing a non-spellcasting craftsperson to work magic into raw materials as the item takes shape.

entries.name: Manufacturing
entries: Despite an adventurer's best efforts, they're probably going to end up with one or two pieces of nonmagical gear. Someone needs to make those items, and the following manufacturing rules put that power in the players' hands.

To manufacture a mundane item, a creature needs three things: materials, a tool, and time. In some cases, at the GM's discretion, a creature might also need auxiliary equipment. Let's take a {@item spear|PHB} as an example.

entries.name: Materials & Cost
entries: As a rule of thumb, the material cost of an item is one-third of its purchase value. Thus a {@item spear|PHB} worth 1 gp requires 3 sp of materials. The GM has great latitude in deciding what these mundane materials might be. The {@table Manufacturing DC & Time|LALoTT} table on pages 20-21 summarises the material cost of different mundane items.
Potion and spell scroll bases are unique in that there is no base item to compare the material cost to. The cost of these magic items' nonmagical materials is 2 gp and 3 gp, respectively.

entries.name: Abilities & Tools
entries: Manufacturing an item requires a tool. The tool required depends on the item's type. The GM has considerable leeway in deciding if a tool is applicable or not, and has the final say on the tool required for any item. In addition, different tools require different abilities to use them. Some tools, like {@item carpenter's tools|PHB}, grant a choice of abilities, much like how Finesse weapons let you choose Strength or Dexterity when making an attack roll. See the {@table Tools and Their Products|LALoTT} table on the next page for a breakdown of tools, abilities, and item types.

entries.name: Tool Proficiency
entries: Proficiency with a tool isn't necessary to attempt a Crafting check that requires that tool. If a character does not have the required proficiency, it can still make the check, albeit with {@b disadvantage}. At the GM's discretion, this disadvantage can be avoided if the character receives guidance from a book or a creature with the requisite proficiency.

entries.name: Difficulty & Flavour
entries: Mundane items have a manufacturing DC associated with their item type; the more complex the item, the higher the DC. See the {@table Manufacturing DC & Time|LALoTT} table on page 20-21 for the full breakdown.

entries.name: Check
entries: To manufacture an item, a creature must gather the required equipment, materials, and tools, and spend the requisite time crafting the item. This time need not be continuous. At the end of the crafting time, a creature makes a Manufacturing check using proficiency with the appropriate tool. A success on this check results in a completed item. A failure results in a non-functioning item or, if you use the quirks optional rule from Heliana's Guide, a functioning item with one or more flaws.

entries.name: Spear Manufacturing
entries: {@b Materials:} 3 sp steel ingots & wood
{@b Time:} 1 hour
{@b Tools:} {@item carpenter's tools|PHB}, {@item smith's tools|PHB}, or {@item woodcarver's tools|PHB}

{@b Manufacturing Check:} {@dc 14} Strength ({@item carpenter's tools|PHB}, {@item smith's tools|PHB}, or {@item woodcarver's tools|PHB}) or {@dc 14} Dexterity ({@item carpenter's tools|PHB} or {@item woodcarver's tools|PHB})

entries.name: Enchanting
entries: Enchanting involves extracting the magical power from monster components and applying them to mundane items. In practice, this can take the form of physically grafting the components to the item or, for the magically inclined, direct transfer of magical power.

entries.name: Materials
entries: The required materials for enchanting are: a mundane item, a magical component, and an essence. The first two are determined by the item's recipe (see pages 25 - 42), while the latter is determined by the item's rarity (see {@table Enchanting Rarity, DC, and Time|LALoTT|Enchanting Rarity, DC, and Time table} on next page).

entries.name: Essence
entries: While magic items gain their nuances from the monster components used in their creation, the amplitude of the item's power is derived from a substance known as essence. The more rare the item, the more powerful it is, and the more potent the essence required.

If you use a rarer essence than required for a particular item, the item gains the rarity of the essence used, affecting the time and DC required to make it (see below). For example, if you make a {@item helm of telepathy} using a robust essence, it would be rare instead of uncommon, requiring 80 hours instead of 20 hours to craft. At the GM's discretion, the power of the item can be increased accordingly, perhaps by increasing the DC of the saving throws to match the new rarity (from DC 13 to 15), or even adding other effects.

entries.name: Difficulty & Time
entries: The DC and time required for the Enchanting check depend on the rarity of the magic item being crafted and whether or not it has attunement; the rarer the item, the more difficult and time consuming it is to craft. This is broken down in the {@table Enchanting Rarity, DC, and Time|LALoTT} table on the following page. Enchanting doesn't have to be done all in one go—a typical enchanter might only work for 8 hours each day.

entries.name: Spell Scrolls
entries: Spell scrolls are unique in that the creature crafting the spell scroll also needs to know the spell they are crafting. For wizards, this means having the spell in their spellbook; for bards, rangers, sorcerers, tamers, and warlocks, this means having the spell in their list of known spells; and for clerics, druids, and paladins, this means any spell in their list.

entries.name: Skills
entries: As with harvesting, the skill associated with the Enchanting check depends on the type of creature the component was harvested from. So, if you're making a sunsplitter spear, which requires an elemental's core of fire as its component, you would make an ability check using {@skill Arcana}. See the {@table Creature Types and Associated Skills|LALoTT} table for the full breakdown.

Unlike harvesting, only creatures with a spellcasting ability can enchant, as the ability used in the crafting check must be the creature's spellcasting ability (typically Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma). A wizard (whose spellcasting ability is Intelligence) enchanting a spear into a sunsplitter weapon would thus make an Intelligence ({@skill Arcana}) check.

entries.name: Check
entries: To enchant an item, a creature must gather the required materials and spend the requisite time crafting the item. This time need not be continuous. At the end of the crafting time, a creature makes an Enchanting check with its spellcasting ability using proficiency with the appropriate skill. A success on this check results in a completed enchantment. On a failure, the enchantment fails or, if you're using the quirks optional rule from Heliana's Guide, a functional enchantment with one or more flaws. Success or failure, the essence and component are consumed.

entries.name: Sunsplitter Spear Enchanting
entries: {@b Rarity:} uncommon
{@b Materials:} {@item spear|PHB}, elemental core of fire, frail essence
{@b Time:} 20 hours
{@b Enchanting Check:} DC 15 spellcasting ability ({@skill Arcana})

entries.name: Magic Item Recipes

entries: Essence is what gives an item its juice, its raw magical power. To mould that power in a specific way, you need to choose the right monster component. Things a creature used to attack, like teeth and claws, are typically used in offensive enchantments, while defensive items are crafted from components like bones, chitin, and scales. It's not necessary to make the component part of the final item, but I do love my dino-tooth helmet!

The following table catalogues every magic item in this book, {@i L'Arsene's Ledger of Treasure and Trinkets}, as well as the items found and crafted in the companion adventure book, {@i Motes of the Divine}. It is the GM's choice if and how a character knows a magic item recipe. The items are listed alphabetically by type and then name. Here's how to interpret the table:

entries.name: Name
entries: The name of the magic item. Items in {@b bold} are made from boss monster components, which are listed in the Metadata column. To make these items a little more unique, you can decide that the component used to craft the item be harvested from the boss monster.

entries.name: Type (Metadata)
entries: The item's type. If an item has multiple types (such as "rod, staff, or wand"), it is listed under the first type alphabetically ("rods").

A superscript 'W' ({@sup W}) indicates that this is a new weapon type, the rules for which can be found on pages 5-9. The word 'any' is used to denote that the magic item can take the form of multiple types. For example:

items: {@b Any polearm:} halberd, glaive, lance, quarterstaff, spear, twinblade, or pike.
{@b Any hammer:} club, greatclub, light hammer, mace, maul, or warhammer.

entries: You can also take the properties of one magic item and apply it to another. For example, you might want a sunsplitter sword instead of a spear, or a light armour version of a copper cuirass. The art might not make sense, but this will not break your game.

entries.name: Value
entries: The suggested price to purchase the item "off the shelf" in gold pieces. This can vary considerably from the price an adventurer might pay if they sourced all the ingredients separately and is a representation of the demand for that type of item. These prices can, and should, change to fit your world.

entries.name: Rarity
entries: The item's rarity, represented using abbreviations: C, common; U, uncommon; R, rare; V, very rare; L, legendary; A, artifact.
{@b Note:} If an item changes rarity, so too does its save DC (see "Save DC and Attack Modifier" table on page 275).

entries.name: Attunement
entries: Indicates whether an item requires attunement ("Att"), what type of attunement, and if it is consumable ("C"). See "Attunement Options" below for details on new types of attunement. This column uses the following abbreviations: Enh, enhanced; Opt, optional; Req, required. A superscript 'S' ({@sup S}) indicates a creature needs to be a spellcaster and a '+' indicates there are other, specific limitations. Blank ("—") means no attunement is required.

entries.name: Type (Component Details)
entries: The types of creature from which a component must be harvested.

entries.name: Metatag
entries: An optional rule that can grant advantage on Crafting checks or can be a requirement to craft an item. For example, the {@item copper cuirass|LALoTT} calls for a pouch of dragon scales. The metatag indicates the scales should come from a copper dragon. A bolded metatag indicates an original Loot Tavern monster.

entries.name: Component
entries: The specific component(s) required to give a magic item its magical effects. A superscript 'V' ({@sup V}) indicates a volatile component (see optional rules in {@i Heliana's Guide}).

entries.name: Page
entries: The page in this book on which the item, its art, and its mechanics can be found. In the PDF, you can click these numbers to jump to that page, and click the item's name to jump back to the table.

entries.name: Attunement Options
entries: Some items in {@i L'Arsene's Ledger} have new types of attunement: enhanced and optional.

entries.name: Enhanced
entries: Properties described as being 'Enhanced Attunement', labelled 'EA' in the item itself and 'Enh' in the tables, are additional magical features that can be unlocked. Usually, this requires the player to fulfill a prerequisite, such as the completion of a task, or, more simply, accepting some downsides associated with the enhancement. Any other properties the item has (i.e. those not labelled 'EA') require attunement as normal.

entries.name: Optional
entries: Properties described as being 'Optional Attunement', labelled 'OA' in the item itself and 'Opt' in the tables, grant their benefits only when a character attunes to the item. Any other properties the item has (i.e. those not labelled 'OA') are granted to the item's user even if they aren't attuned.

entries.name: Enigmatic Recipes
entries: Several items in this table have components with metatags harvestable from creatures found in a collection of hunts known collectively as {@i Blood of Sun & Shadow}. These are:

Except for {@i Garden of Dark Delights}, which is included as bonus in the appendices of {@i Motes of the Divine}, these hunts are PDF only. You can find them through the Loot Tavern website, which you can access by clicking or scanning the QR code to the right, or by typing in the URL {@link https://heliana.lt/BoSS}.

entries.name: Loot Tables
entries: Whether you want to choose trinkets for the dragon's treasure hoard, populate the contents of a merchant's shop, or randomly determine the prize of a fantasy gashapon machine, these tables are here to help. They follow the same pattern as Magic Item Tables A - I in other 5th-edition rulesets:

items: {@table Magic Item Table A} - common items; attunement, non-attunement, and consumable.
All items are either consumable or, in a few, choicecases non-attunement:
{@table Magic Item Table D} - very rare.
All items are not consumable. The lower numbers are non-attunement, and higher numbers are attunement:
{@table Magic Item Table F} - uncommon items.
{@table Magic Item Table G} - rare items.
{@table Magic Item Table H} - very rare items.
{@table Magic Item Table I} - legendary items.

entries.name: Choosing Tables
entries: You can use these new tables, in addition to those in other 5e books. To do so, roll the die in the left hand column (typically a d4 or a d10) of each table when you roll the d100. If the result is one of the options in the table below, consult the tables in other 5e books. Otherwise, use the specified table in this book.

entries.name: Rarity Variants
entries: As explained in Chapter 4: Magic Items, this book contains rarity variants—differing versions of the same items with effects balanced at those rarities. Instructions on how to change the effects of a magic item when using a rarity variant are written at the bottom of each item.

entries.name: Boss Items
entries: As in the previous section, item names in {@b bold} are crafted from boss monster components.

entries.name: Pages
entries: The number in this column indicates on which page the item's text can be found.

entries.name: Tables A2-E2
entries: The items in these tables cover all consumable items, progressing in rarity from common to legendary in tables A to E, respectively. There are also a couple of choice, low power non-attunement items. In all cases, you can choose between these tables and those in the core 5e books by rolling a d4; on a 1-2, use the tables in the core 5e books, on a 3-4, use the tables here.

entries.name: Tables F2-F4
entries: The items in these tables cover non-consumable items of uncommon rarity. When you roll to decide on the item, also roll a d4 and consult the table to the right to determine which table to use.

entries.name: Tables G2-G5
entries: The items in these tables cover non-consumable items of rare rarity. When you roll to decide on the item, also roll a d10 and consult the table below to determine which table to use.

entries.name: Tables H2-H5
entries: The items in these tables cover non-consumable items of very rare rarity. When you roll to decide on the item, also roll a d10 and consult the table below to determine which table to use.

entries.name: Table I2
entries: The items in this table covers non-consumable items of legendary rarity. When you roll to decide on the item, also roll a d4 and consult the table below to determine which table to use.

data.name: Chapter 4: Magic Items

entries: We had an odd assortment of individuals pass through the tavern recently. I think they were druids, as they kept going on about watching each other on the 'Yew Tube'. Anyway, they inspired me to artifice an interesting array of trinkets in a theme I call 'Patrons' of Legend'.

Magic items can range from adventuring essentials to jokey trinkets, from aesthetic baubles to character-defining treasures, and from cursed heirlooms to world-shaping artifacts. In this chapter you will find the 325 magic items that comprise this ledger! Each is built around a nugget of lore that you can weave into your world or entirely ignore.

entries.name: Themes
entries: The items in this chapter are divided into themes: groups of items that work thematically together. The themes are organised chronologically, in the order in which they were created. Within a theme, items are arranged alphabetically.

entries.name: Rarity Variants
entries: This book uses item rarity as a barometer of the item's power; the rarer the item, the more powerful it is. In general, more powerful items should be given to player characters as they reach higher levels. Most of the items in this section come with rarity variants, versions of the item balanced for different rarities.

Rarity variants allow you to present a low-level character with an item around which they can build their roleplay or combat strategies. Then, as the character levels up, youcan offer ways for the character to improve the item: quests, craftspeople, and the upgrade rules in Heliana's Guide. This increases the item's rarity (and power), allowing it to remain a useful item for that character.

items.name: Jade Skull of the Sun King

data.name: Chapter 5: Crafting Workshop

entries: So, you want to design yer own items do you? Are you familiar with the laws of arcanodynamics? The conservation of magic? The principles of essential attunement and power modality? Aye... well neither am I. Ask L'Arsène or Humperdink. I'm just the huntress.

Designing a magic item can be a perilous process. How much extra damage is too much? What's an appropriate save DC? Will allowing a creature to cast {@spell entangle} at will make your deadly combat encounters as soft as Peeper's belly? (Hint: yes, it will).

This section dives into how to balance magic items using Crafting Points (CP) and the unwritten (until now) rules of item design. It is intended to assist GMs in creating uncommon to very rare items, though points breakdowns for all rarities are provided. The options presented herein form a basis on which you can build myriad magical mysteries.

entries.name: Anatomy of a Magic Item
entries: Items in this book convey three different types of information: metadata, flavour, and mechanics. Metadata includes the item name, type, rarity, attunement requirements, and crafting components. Flavour is usually conveyed in the first paragraph, and can detail anything from the item's appearance and other sensory qualities to its lore. Lastly, and most importantly for this section, are mechanics, which are broken down into properties and effects. A single property has one or more effects. Properties with multiple effects typically have their own subtitle, such as "Flame Flinger" in the example below.

entries.name: Ring of the Pyromancer
entries: This ring has two properties; the first is resistance to fire damage and the second is a charge-based property. The charge-based property has two effects, casting either {@spell flaming sphere} or {@spell wall of fire}.

entries.name: Ring of the Pyromancer
entries: {@i Ring, very rare (requires attunement)}
Crafted from the feathers of a phoenix, this ruby-encrusted ring is always warm to the touch.
While wearing this ring, you have resistance to fire damage. This ring has 3 charges and regains all expended charges daily at dawn.

entries.name: Flame Flinger
entries: As an action while wearing the ring, you can expend 1 or more charges to cast the following spells (save {@dc 16}): {@spell flaming sphere} ({@b 1 charge}) or {@spell wall of fire} ({@b 2 charges}).

entries.name: From Concept to Creation
entries: Before you determine the details of your magic item, it's essential to first have a rough idea of its function. Is it a weapon designed to slay formidable foes? Perhaps it's a versatile tool to aid in exploration, or a protective charm that shields its bearer from harmful magic? Consider how the item fits within your world; the most memorable magic items have unique or compelling purposes. Write this down! Now you are ready to decide properties and calculate crafting points.

entries.name: Crafting Points
entries: Crafting Points (CP) are the unit by which we'll measure the power of a magic item. The typical number of CP that an item can have depends on its rarity and whether or not it requires attunement. The table below describes the ideal CP ranges for items of different rarities and attunements, as well as consumable items—items which are destroyed after a short number of uses.
Usually, it's acceptable to deviate a few points outside the range if there's no other way to make the item you want, but try to go no more than a 10 CP difference.

entries.name: Choosing Item Properties
entries: To create a magic item, choose which properties it has (for example, the ability to deal extra damage or frighten an enemy creature) and add up each property's CP cost, making sure that the total CP value is within the range for its intended rarity. To make it easier for you to find the properties you want for your item, they are organised in 4 sections: {@b Offensive}, {@b Defensive}, {@b Spells}, and {@b Utility}. Additionally, there are a few rules and concepts that you have to bear in mind: rarity restrictions and the number of properties.

entries.name: Rarity Restrictions
entries: Some effects are simply too powerful for a certain rarity of item, even if they fit within the CP range. For example, a 5th-level spell is worth 56 CP (see page 280), so it could fit within the budget of an uncommon item that requires attunement (51-75 CP). However, players are expected to find uncommon items at early levels, while 5th-level spells are typically acquired at 9th level or higher; it's rarely a good idea to make the powerful effects of 5th-level spells available at early levels.

To prevent this, many effects are restricted to items of a certain rarity or above. In the sections listing the properties that you can choose for your items, this is denoted through the use of colour-coding as seen in the Number of Properties by Rarity table below.

entries.name: Number of Properties
entries: More properties means more flexibility, which means more power. Therefore, lower-rarity items should have fewer properties. This also helps newer, often lower-level players from being overwhelmed by items with too many options. In addition, if you give an item the ability to cast spells, the number of spell options available should be proportionate to the item's rarity. You can use the following table as guidance.

entries.name: Action Economy
entries: Properties can be broadly divided into two categories: active (requiring the user to activate its effects) and passive (the effects are always on, requiring no input from the user). Generally, active properties require the use of an action, bonus action, or reaction, which affects their CP value (listed in their respective tables).

entries.name: Determining Action Economy
entries: When should a property require you to use an action, bonus action, or reaction? When is it alright to require none at all? As a rule of thumb, an item's action economy will be balanced by the CP value assigned to it, but it's good to keep some basic principles in mind nonetheless:

items.name: Action
entries: A property should require an action when it has a large impact on the game, such as dealing large amounts of damage, inflicting crippling conditions, or creating powerful areas of effect (AoEs).

items.name: Bonus Action
entries: A property should require a bonus action when it has a smaller impact, such as inflicting light damage or healing minor amounts of hit points. Bonus actions are also fantastic for set-up abilities (for example, using a bonus action so that your next attack that turn deals extra damage).

items.name: Reaction
entries: A property should require a reaction when it has a smaller impact, similar to a bonus action. More importantly, a reaction is always taken in response to something else happening.

items.name: No Action
entries: Normally, active properties should require an action, bonus action, or reaction to activate. Err on the side of caution and try to avoid active properties that require no action. However, it's alright to create properties that don't require any action to activate as long as they follow these restrictions:

items: They have a small impact on the game, such as dealing minor amounts of damage. {@damage 1d6} damage to a single creature is alright, but {@damage 3d6} damage in an AoE is not.

There is always a prerequisite that must be fulfilled prior to the property being able to be activated. For example, hitting a creature, reducing a creature to 0 hit points, failing a saving throw, or scoring a critical hit are all valid prerequisites. Guaranteed effects are bad (See "Pitfalls to Avoid", page 281). You shouldn't be able to simply expend 1 charge with no action, bonus action, or reaction to deal {@damage 1d6} damage to a creature. However, allowing you to expend 1 charge to have your magic weapon deal an extra {@damage 1d6} damage when you hit a creature with it is fine.

entries.name: Attunement
entries: Except in specific cases, a player character can attune to only three magic items; a character's attunement slots are a very valuable resource! Accordingly, attunement requirements deeply affect an item's balance, and deciding whether or not an item requires attunement is an integral part of creating a new magic item. See the sidebar "Rules Reminder: Attunement" for a refresher.
An item's CP value isn't the only factor that determines whether or not it should require attunement. There are two additional aspects to bear in mind when answering this question:

items.name: Shareability
entries: If an item could easily be passed between party members outside of combat so that everyone shares its benefits, it should probably require attunement. Consider the following scenario: the party encounters a trap that spits gouts of flame, and they have a ring that doesn't require attunement and grants immunity to fire damage. A party member can wear the ring to cross the trap, ignore the fire damage, then throw it back to another party member, who repeats the process. When making a magic item, try to avoid enabling scenarios like this.

items.name: Stacking
entries: If an item provides a common, desirable, and stackable bonus (such as a +1 bonus to AC), think carefully about whether or not it should require attunement. +1 armor doesn't, true, but a character can only wear one piece of armour at a time. If you create, for example, a ring that grants a +1 bonus to AC, however, and doesn't require attunement, it becomes very easy to begin accumulating these bonuses to inflate one's AC beyond what is reasonable.
For your convenience, properties that require attunement regardless of CP have been labelled as such in their respective sections.

entries.name: Optional & Enhanced Attunement
entries: This book introduces two new forms of attunement. Items with optional attunement provide a smaller modicum of power when used by a creature to whom they are not attuned, and have their full potential unlocked upon attunement. Items with enhanced attunement always require attunement, and become stronger if the creature attuned to it fulfils certain requisites. Both of these kinds of items should be balanced taking into account their maximum potential. For example, a rare item with optional attunement should be within 101-125 CP when a creature is attuned to it, and the properties that don't require attunement can have a combined CP value than is recommended for a rare item that doesn't require attunement (76-100 CP).

entries.name: Rules Reminder: Attunement
entries: Some magic items require a creature to form a bond with them before their magical properties can be used; this bond is called attunement. Without becoming attuned to an item that requires attunement, a creature gains only its nonmagical benefits, unless its description states otherwise.

Attuning to an item requires a creature to spend a short rest focused on only that item while being in physical contact with it. An item can be attuned to by only one creature at a time, and a creature can be attuned to no more than three magic items at a time. Additionally, a creature can't attune to more than one copy of an item. For example, a creature can't attune to more than one {@item ring of protection} at a time.

A creature's attunement to an item ends if the creature no longer satisfies the prerequisites for attunement, if the item has been more than 100 feet away for at least 24 hours, if the creature dies, or if another creature attunes to the item. A creature can also voluntarily end attunement by spending a short rest focused on the item, unless the item is cursed.

entries.name: Limited Uses
entries: In most cases, items with active properties allow you to use them a limited number of times. Two common ways of implementing this are charges and uses per day.

entries.name: Charges
entries: Charges are a pool of resources from which one or more properties can draw. If an item has 4 or fewer total charges, it generally regains all expended charges, and if the total charges are higher than 4, it regains a random number. In both cases, this usually happens at dawn.

When multiple properties use an item's charges, calculate the CP cost per charge for each effect and identify which has the highest cost per charge. Next, multiply the highest cost per charge by the item's maximum number of charges to decide the CP value of all the effects/properties that use charges. See the ring of the pyromancer example on the next page for an illustration of how this might work.

entries.name: Uses per Day
entries: Properties that can be used a certain number of times per day, usually recharging at dawn, work independently from one another. Using one doesn't affect how many times you can use the other, so you calculate each property's CP cost separately.

entries.name: Example: Ring of the Pyromancer
entries: This example begins with a 4 charge version of the item on page 271, before balancing it by removing a charge. Firstly, the ring imparts resistance to fire damage (see page 278).
Next is the charge-based property, in this case Flame Flinger, which lets you cast spells (see page 280). We must work out the cost per charge:

entries.text: As an action you can cast the {@spell flaming sphere} spell for 1 charge, or the {@spell wall of fire} spell for 2 charges. {@spell Flaming sphere} is a 2nd-level spell so has a CP cost of 26, while {@spell wall of fire} is 4th-level spell, so costs 44 CP (see the CP Cost of Spells table on page 280). Because {@spell wall of fire} uses 2 charges, its CP cost per charge (CP/c) is 22, while {@spell flaming sphere} has a CP/c of 26.

entries: Now we work out how many CP all 4 charges use:

entries.text: In terms of the CP cost, using the ring to cast {@spell flaming sphere} is superior to using it to cast {@spell wall of fire}. Even if there are situations in which it might be better to cast the latter, we will exclusively use the CP/c cost of {@spell flaming sphere}, and multiply it by the item's maximum number of charges to get the CP cost: 4 x 26 = 104. Therefore, the charge-based property of the item costs 104 CP.

entries: Finally, we sum the cost of all the properties:

entries.text: The total CP value of the ring is 90 + 104 = 194 CP.

entries: Considering only its CP value, this item would be legendary and require no attunement (181-210 CP). However, as the ring provides damage resistance that would be easily shareable, attunement should be required for this item (see page 273). Because making it a legendary item that requires attunement would make it too weak, we can settle on very rare with attunement (151-180 CP). Reducing the number of charges to 3 would subtract 26 CP and make the total CP value for the ring 168—perfect!

entries.name: Multiple Targets and Areas of Effect
entries: Many different properties can be made to affect multiple creatures or even an entire area instead of a single creature. For example, you might want to make an item that deals force damage in a line or restores hit points to every ally in a sphere.
The size of AoEs (Areas of Effect) depends on the rarity of the item, as detailed in the AoE Sizes by Rarity table below.

entries.name: Converting Single-Target Properties
entries: In order to convert a property that affects a single creature into one that affects multiple, take its original CP cost and add the value in the respective "No Action or Extra Creature" column for each additional creature affected beyond the first. As a rule, AoEs are assumed to affect two creatures. For example, using an action to restrain two creatures for 1 minute (with repeated saves) has a cost of 9 + 30 = 39 CP per use. The CP Cost of Active Damage Bonuses table (page 276) includes the values for AoEs, for convenience.

entries.name: Save DC and Attack Modifiers
entries: Some items may have properties that require the target to make a saving throw, or that require the user to make a special attack. When this is the case, the save DC or attack modifier depends on the item's rarity, as detailed in the table below:

entries.caption: Save DC & Attack Modifier by Rarity

entries.name: Sight and Range
entries: When should the user be required to see the target of one of your item's properties in order to use it? How far should an effect reach?

entries.name: Sight
entries: Generally, properties that target a point in space (usually to create an area of effect) don't require sight. Similarly, properties that require you to make an attack roll also don't require sight—you're already penalised if you can't see the target by gaining disadvantage on the attack roll. Most other properties should require sight to be used.

entries.name: Range
entries: Range is the distance away from the user at which an effect takes place. For an AoE, this is the maximum distance away from the user at which the effect is centred. Range doesn't usually play a significant part in its balance. Most combat takes place within 60 feet of a character, so 60 feet is a good choice for most ranged effects. Increasing it a little more is fitting for effects that should be especially far-reaching, but try not to exceed 120 feet and exercise caution.

entries.name: Negative Values
entries: Some properties have a CP cost of 1 or lower, often running into the negatives. This means that it's usually not worth using that property over simply attacking as normal. If a property has a negative cost, do not use it in your item; it will inflate the CP budget and throw balance askew. These costs are given anyway to allow for the calculation of effects that target multiple creatures (see "Multiple Targets and Areas of Effect", page 274).

entries.name: Offensive Properties
entries: These properties allow a magic item's user to deal damage, hit with attacks more reliably, or impose debilitating conditions on enemies. They are often applied to weapons.

entries.name: Attack and Damage Bonuses
entries: One of the most common and undeniably useful properties a magic weapon can have is an additional bonus to attack and damage rolls made with it, or the ability to passively deal extra damage on a hit. The two tables below provide the CP costs for these properties.

entries.caption: CP Cost of Passive Offensive Bonuses
CP Cost of Passive Damage Bonuses

entries.name: Damage per Use
entries: Sometimes, you might want to give an item a property that deals additional damage with a limited number of uses. As described, the CP cost depends on the action required to activate the effect. Note that the cost in the following table is per use; if the property costs 1 charge to activate and you have 5 charges, the CP cost of the property is five times the per-use cost.

entries.name: Grey Values
entries: If the value on the table is greyed out (represented here as negative values), it means that it's usually not worth it to spend the indicated action economy on that property.

entries.caption: CP Cost of Active Damage Bonuses
footnotes: *Does this look odd? See the "CP Discrepancy" sidebar on the next page for an explanation.

entries.name: Conditions
entries: Detrimental conditions can be very powerful in a fight. They can grant creatures advantage to hit their foes while imposing disadvantage on the foes' attacks. In general, conditions require a saving throw and last until the end of the target's next turn, or for 1 minute while allowing the target to repeat the saving throw at the end of each of its turns. In this latter case, we consider a condition to last for an average of two rounds.

entries.name: Exhaustion and Petrification
entries: The table has no values for the exhaustion and petrified conditions. They have been purposefully omitted as they are extremely powerful and difficult to balance.

entries.name: Multiple Targets
entries: The CP costs in the table below are used when an effect imposes a condition on a single creature. To apply conditions to affect an additional target, see page 274.

entries.name: Grey Values
entries: If the value on the table is greyed out (represented here as low or negative values), it means that it's usually not worth it to spend the indicated action economy on that property. Do not use them unless you want to convert the effect into one that affects multiple targets, making the value positive.

entries.caption: CP Cost of Conditions

colLabelRows.entry: Repeat Save at End of Turn (2 Rounds)

footnotes: *Including on an attack hit
**This is a beneficial condition; it typically applies to willing, friendly creatures without a saving throw.
***An instantaneous effect without a duration or repeated saving throw

entries.name: CP Discrepancy
entries: You may have noticed an oddity in some of the damage values presented in the "CP Cost of Active Damage Bonuses" and "CP Cost of Healing & THP" tables. For example, why is the value for 4d10 single-target damage as an action 12.5 but the value for 5d8 single-target damage as an action much less, at 1.5? 5d8 is more than 4d10, so shouldn't it be higher?

This is because these two values are intended to be used at different rarities, and the impact of a character's action is weighed differently at different points in their progression. From 4th to 5th level, player characters receive their biggest power bump: martial characters gain access to Extra Attack, and full spellcasters gain their first 3rd-level spell slots and spells like {@spell fireball}. While {@spell burning hands}' 3d6 AoE damage as an action may seem impressive early on, by the time a martial character gets their first rare item, they can probably deal as much damage or more with an Attack action, without needing to spend any resources.

For an uncommon item, the CP cost of 5d8 single-target damage as an action would be higher than 1.5, but that amount of damage is restricted to rare rarity. Similarly, for a rare item, the value of 4d10 single-target damage as an action would be lower, to the point where it would probably not be worth an action.

entries.name: Defensive Properties
entries: Defensive properties allow a magic item's user to avoid taking damage, mitigate damage, or regain hit points. They can be applied to any type of item, but are most common on armour and wearable wondrous items.

entries.name: Armour Class
entries: A passive bonus to AC is a common staple of defensive magic items, bolstering a creature's defence by making them harder to hit. The table below lists the CP costs for each bonus.

entries.name: Damage Resistances
entries: The table below lists the CP costs for passive resistance to certain damage types, according to how common these are among monsters in the game. For example, fire and poison are some of the most commonly found damage types, whereas cold is slightly more uncommon, and every other type is relatively rare. It might be wise to adjust these values if your campaign setting distributes damage types differently. Items with these properties always require attunement.

The values of resistance to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage are not included. Because of how ubiquitous those damage types are, permanent resistance to any of them risks breaking the balance of the game.

entries.name: Healing & Temporary Hit Points
entries: Do you enjoy dying? No? Then you might need to restore some of those lost hit points, or avoid losing them in the first place with some temporary hit points (THP).

entries.name: Multiple Targets
entries: Activated effects that heal or grant temporary hit points can be made to affect multiple targets (see page 274 for more information).

entries.name: Outside of Combat
entries: Just like damage, certain amounts of THP or healing might not be worth an action. However, healing and THP differ from damage in the fact that they can be used outside of combat, making the action economy irrelevant. Because of that, when using healing or THP in an item, always use the "No Action or Extra Creature" value regardless of their action economy, unless at least one of the following is true:

items: In the case of THP, they last for 1 minute or less. Because they expire so rapidly, the user will likely need to gain them in battle, making the action economy relevant again.

The healing or THP has a condition attached that only makes the property usable in battle. For example, an item might have a property that allows you to heal yourself for 2d6 hit points as a reaction to being hit by an attack.

entries.name: Grey Values
entries: If the value on the table is greyed out (represented here as low or negative values), it means that it's usually not worth it to spend the respective action economy on that property. Do not use them unless you want to convert the effect into one that affects multiple targets, making the value positive.

entries.caption: CP Cost of Healing & THP
footnotes: *Does this look odd? See the "CP Discrepancy" sidebar on page 277 for an explanation.

entries.name: Saving Throws
entries: Gaining advantage on a saving throw is a very powerful ability that can protect a magic item's user from some of the nastiest effects the game has to offer.

entries.name: Multiple Targets
entries: Activated effects that grant advantage on a saving throw can be made to affect multiple targets (see page 274 for more information).

entries.caption: CP Cost of Saving Throw Advantage
footnotes: *Items with these properties ALWAYS require attunement.

entries.name: Spells
entries: Due to how numerous and diverse they are, spells are some of the best ways to add power and flavour to your magic items. The following table gives a CP cost for each casting of a spell based on the level of the spell.

Remember: unless specified otherwise, spells cast from an item are cast at the lowest possible level and use their regular casting time. Cantrips cast from an item don't scale unless the item explicitly states they do.

entries.caption: CP Cost of Spells
footnotes: *Never include magic missile or shield as spells that can be cast at will from an item.
**If the cantrip doesn't scale, this is worth 10 CP. If it scales with player level, it is worth 21 CP instead.

entries.name: Modifying Spell Level
entries: Some of the values for spells may not correspond to the spell's actual power; spells like {@spell fireball} and {@spell lightning bolt} are deliberately overpowered and actually do more damage than a 3rd-level spell should. Furthermore, spells like {@spell entangle}, which applies a debilitating condition (restrained) across a large area of effect, are also more powerful than their level would indicate. In order to maintain balance between spells and other effects, treat certain spells as having a different effective level (and thus CP cost) than their spell level would indicate. Those spells are presented below:

entries.name: Utility
entries: This section is dedicated to additional effects that don't fit in the previous three categories, such as additional proficiencies, advantage on ability checks, or extra weapon properties.

entries.name: Skills & Ability Checks
entries: Don't underestimate the power of ability checks! An opportune use of Stealth or a clever instance of Persuasion might mean the difference between life and death. Skills and tools are divided below according to how often they are generally used, which lists the CP costs for proficiency in the skill or advantage on ability checks using that skill.

entries.caption: CP Cost of Ability Check Bonuses
rows: Arcana, Medicine, Sleight of Hand, Survival, all tools
Animal Handling, History, Nature, Performance, Religion, gaming set, musical instrument
footnotes: *A creature shouldn't have proficiency in initiative rolls.

entries.name: Others
entries: Magic items can have a whole plethora of effects. Unfortunately, describing them all could fill a whole book, but a few extra effects that might come in handy have been described in the table below, along with their CP costs.

entries.caption: CP Cost of Other Properties
rows: Additional beneficial weapon property (e.g. Finesse, Light, Reach, Thrown)
Climbing or swimming speed of 30 feet or equal to walking speed
Climbing or swimming speed of 60 feet

entries.name: Pitfalls to Avoid
entries: Some features are just too powerful, and break game balance even if they fit the CP range for the item. When making magic items, avoid the following:

entries.name: Bag of Rats
entries: Avoid effects that can be triggered by reducing a creature to 0 hit points without implementing some other sort of limit. A player can easily break an effect like this by having their character carry around a bag of rats (or other weak creatures) to trigger them whenever they please. Requiring the creature to be of a certain size (Small or larger) or a minimum CR helps prevent this exploit.

entries.name: Damage Immunity
entries: Damage immunity is a very powerful effect. Despite the fact that it exists in some of the items in the SRD, we advise steering clear of it due to the potential it has to deeply affect the game's balance.

entries.name: Guaranteed Effects
entries: In general, no effect should automatically be applied to a target, unless the target is willing. This means there always has to be some event, such as hitting with an attack or failing a saving throw, before a target becomes debilitated or takes damage. The exception to this rule is effects that still take place even on a successful saving throw, which can be limited in a different fashion (see next paragraph).

entries.name: Unlimited, Guaranteed Damage
entries: It is an unwritten rule within 5th edition that no class feature, magical effect, or item property should permit a player to deal guaranteed damage without having some other sort of limit. For example, effects that deal "half damage on a failed save" should never be able to be used "at will". Instead, they should either deal no damage on a failed saving throw (like cantrips), or have a limited number of uses per day, either by expending uses of features like Channel Divinity, recharging on a rest, or expending an item's charges

entries.name: Putting Your Item Together
entries: You have your idea for an item and a list of properties to pick from. All you need to do now is to put them all together! Here's an example of how this would progress.

entries.name: Step 1 - Metadata and CP Range
entries: Select the item's type, its rarity, and whether or not it requires attunement. Alternatively, you can complete steps 2 and 3 below and then determine the item's metadata based on the item's CP value.

entries.name: Example
entries: Let's say that we want to make a rare shortbow that doesn't require attunement. Our CP range for this item is 76-100 CP, as shown on the "CP Range by Rarity" table on page 271.

entries.name: Step 2 - Desired Properties
entries: Choose the effects you want the item to have. Make sure that the item's rarity and attunement is appropriate for that type of effect. Determine the values of any save DCs or attack bonuses the item might require from the "Save DC & Attack Modifier by Rarity" table on page 275. If the item creates an AoE, determine its size from the "AoE Sizes by Rarity" table on page 274.

entries.name: Example
entries: We want our bow to be themed around fear, so let's give it some passive psychic damage on each hit. To further this concept, we could add some type of terrifying effect, so how about shooting an arrow that releases a banshee's scream, inflicting the frightened condition in an area for 1 minute?

We want this to affect creatures in a sphere; the table on page 274 tells us this area should have a 15-foot radius. The table on page 275 dictates a save DC of 15 for rare items. The table on page 277 suggests a Wisdom saving throw for the frightened condition. This is a strong effect, so let's make it take an action and give the item some charges, requiring the user to expend 1 charge to use this property. We'll let this ability take place within the normal range of your weapon.

entries.name: Step 3 - Calculating CP
entries: After we calculate the CP cost for each property, we can add them all together and see if it falls in the bounds of a rare, non-attunement magic item (76-100 CP). If the final CP value is outside the range for your chosen rarity and attunement, adjust either the metadata (Step 1) or the effects (Step 2) until they're in balance.

entries.name: Example
entries: We want two things: extra damage on a hit and a horrifying effect that frightens foes.

entries.name: Extra damage:
entries: The lowest we can go is 1d4 extra damage per hit, which is already worth 75 CP—quite a big chunk of our 76-100 CP budget! In this case, it might be better for us to return to Step 2 and rethink this. Maybe the bow only deals extra damage once on each of your turns. If that's the case, we can pick 1d6, which is worth 52.5 CP.

entries.name: Shrieking shot:
entries: The frightened condition as an action (with a repeated save at the end of turn) is worth -3 CP. To make it into an AoE, we add the value on the "No Action or Extra Creature" column (again, with a repeated save at the end of its turn). -3 + 18 = 15 CP. Let's say the bow has 3 charges, giving us a final CP cost of 15 x 3 = 45 CP.

entries.name: Total CP:
entries: Add them together: 52.5 + 45 = 97.5 CP. This is within our CP range!

entries.name: Step 4 - Flavour and Lore
entries: All that's left now is coming up with the name of your item and a brief description for it! What does the item look like? Why was it first created? Does it have any history? Who used it in the past, and how?

It might also be a good idea to add a little context to your properties, so that it's clear how you are managing to do something (in our example, you are frightening creatures by shooting an arrow that emits a scream).

entries.name: Banshee's Bow
entries: {@i Weapon (shortbow), rare}
It is said that the spirit of a mourning banshee lingers in this weapon, eager to share its sorrow with the world. Sometimes, in the dead of night, soft weeping can be heard coming from the bow.
This bow deals an extra 1d6 psychic damage to the first target it hits on each of your turns. It has 3 charges and regains all expended charges daily at dawn.

entries.name: Shrieking Shot
entries: As an action while holding this bow, you can expend 1 charge to shoot a spectral arrow at a point within the normal range of your weapon. The arrow emits a blood-curdling scream on impact and remains embedded in the surface for the next minute. Each creature in a 15-foot-radius sphere centred on that point must succeed on a DC 15 Wisdom saving throw or be {@condition frightened} of the arrow for the duration. A creature can repeat this saving throw at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success
