entries.name: Advanced Rule: Combining Properties
entries: The properties listed so far are only a simple base for you to build on. Many of them can be re-flavoured and combined in different ways, allowing you to create brand new properties for your magic items. Don't be afraid to unleash your creativity and experiment, but remember that creating homebrew content always has the chance of upsetting game balance. It's alright to tweak and rebalance your items after you've created them!

entries.name: CP per Turn
entries: When allowing multiple effects to happen on a single turn, you might end up with a property that outputs a lot of CP worth of effects in one burst. In this case, even if the CP is within the item's budget, the property might be too powerful. You can avoid this by ensuring that the maximum amount of CP that a property can output per turn doesn't exceed the values in the table below.

entries.name: Example: Leeching Wand
entries: Here is an example of an item built by combining several different properties into a new, unique one.

entries.name: Step 1 - Metadata and CP Range
entries: e want this to be an uncommon wand that requires attunement. Our range is 51-75 CP.

entries.name: Step 2 - Desired Effects
entries: This wand will have a single property, but it will be rather complex. This property will expend charges to place a debilitating curse on a foe, allowing the user to drain the target of its life to invigorate the user.

We'll represent this curse using the {@condition poisoned} condition. It'll take an action to use, require a Constitution saving throw, and last for 1 minute with a repeated saving throw. Because of our rarity, the save DC for this will be 13.

In addition, while the creature is {@condition poisoned} in this way, we'll give the wand's user the ability to use a bonus action to force the creature to make another Constitution saving throw, dealing some damage to it—necrotic damage can represent it wasting away—and regaining hit points equal to half the damage dealt.

entries.name: Step 3 - Calculating CP
entries: Now we calculate the CP for our curse.

entries.name: Life-draining curse:
entries: As an action, apply the {@condition poisoned} condition to a single target for 1 minute with repeated saves. The value for this is -7 CP. Normally, we shouldn't use these negative values, but by the time we're finished, this property will be worth a lot more!

entries.name: Necrotic damage:
entries: As a bonus action, deal 2d8 necrotic damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one—5.5 CP. Additionally, when a condition lasts for 1 minute or until the affected creature succeeds on its saving throw, it's expected to last for 2 turns; you can take this bonus action twice on separate turns! 5.5 x 2 = 11 CP.

entries.name: Healing:
entries: Finally, we heal for half of the necrotic damage: 1d8. Healing like this doesn't take additional action economy, so use the CP cost for 1d8 healing on the "No Action or Extra Creature" column: 9 CP. We already know that the user can deal the necrotic damage twice, so the user can heal twice as well. 9 x 2 = 18 CP.

entries.name: CP Cost per Charge:
entries: Now we sum the costs of all the effects: -7 + 11 + 18 = 22 CP. Note that the final value for our property is above the Max CP Expendable per Turn. However, this effect takes place over the course of two turns, so the CP/turn is 11 CP—an acceptable value.

entries.name: Total CP:
entries: Let's give the item 3 charges and make the user expend 1 charge to use this property. 3 x 22 = 66 CP, which is within the correct CP range!

entries.name: Step 4 - Flavour and Lore
entries: Once we add the finishing touches, the final result might look something like this:

entries.name: Leeching Wand
entries: {@i Wand, uncommon (requires attunement)}
This sinister wand is made of dark, gnarled wood, and seems to drain the life force of those who touch it. The wand has 3 charges and regains all expended charges daily at dawn.

entries.name: Life-Draining Curse
entries: As an action while holding the wand, you can expend 1 charge to attempt to curse a creature you can see within 30 feet of you. The creature must succeed on a DC 13 Constitution saving throw or be {@condition poisoned} for 1 minute. The creature can repeat the saving throw at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on a success.

As a bonus action you can drain the vitality of a creature within 30 feet of you that is cursed in this way. The creature must make a DC 13 Constitution saving throw, taking 2d8 necrotic damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a success. You regain hit points equal to half the necrotic damage dealt.

data.name: Chapter 6: Subclasses

entries: All sorts pass through taverns. Vagabonds, adventurers, runaway princesses heading to another castle. But some folk can call a tavern home; they have its beer-soaked dirt under their nails and bar nut oil greasing their foreheads.

Taverns bring folk from all walks of life together. Whether it be to shelter from a passing forcestorm, play roulette and eat a 'bona fide meat pie', or win coin using a deck of tricksy mimic cards. With new faces comes new information, and those who know how to charm the drunk and disorderly can often come away from such interactions furnished with a suite of new rumours. This chapter provides bar-themed subclass options for the barbarian, bard, cleric, fighter, monk, rogue, and tamer.

entries.name: Tamer Class
entries: The monster tamer is a class introduced in {@i Heliana's Guide to Monster Hunting}. It can capture creatures in specially- made vessels, become companions as it bonds with them, and then fight alongside them in battle. To get a free PDF of the {@class tamer|HelianasGuidetoMonsterHunting} class and one of its subclasses, scan or click the QR code below, or type in the URL.
{@link heliana.lt⁄Tamer|heliana.lt⁄Tamer}

data.name: Chapter 7: Familiars

entries: When that over-dressed, nuisance-creating, biomancytouting blood sucker, Humperdink, snuck pages into my last masterpiece, L'Arsene's Crafting Catalogue, I wasn't right impressed. But after gifting him cursed pants that set on fire whenever he so much as exaggerated, I think we're evens. Besides, it turns out people quite liked his additions.

My favourite of this batch is the anveleon. I recently persuaded the tavern to create an air duct between my smithy and Humperdink's lab. I like to leave the anveleon right next to a vent and let it hammer away, crafting spoons throughout the night. I even gave a whole set of silver cutlery for his birthday; I've never seen him so pale! Wait till I give him a garlycanthrope for Giftmas...

Within this chapter you'll find three bonus familiars that can be crafted and then summoned by any character with the find familiar spell as a 'bespoke familiar'. In addition, each of these familiars comes replete with bespoke tech trees, granting additional options tamers (a class introduced in Heliana's Guide) can spend their companion's improvements on. A GM can even choose to grant these improvements to a player character's familiar as a small power boost.

entries.name: Crafting Familiars
entries: To craft a bespoke familiar, Humperdink (or another biomancer) needs one of the unique monster's components, a drop of the player character's blood, and a fee equal to 20 times the player character's level in gold pieces. After 24 hours, the familiar is crafted and is immediately bonded to the character from whom the blood was donated. When that character casts the find familiar spell, they can choose this crafted familiar instead of one of the normal options.

entries.name: Pact of the Chain
entries: If you're using a bespoke familiar as the familiar for a warlock's Pact of the Chain, you might want to make it slightly more powerful so that it compares with the other available options. To do so, simply give it an extra three Hit Dice (and adjust its hit points accordingly), the 1st-level improvement on its Improvement Tree, and your choice of one of the 3rd-level improvements.

entries.name: Tamers
entries: Don't have {@i Heliana's Guide?} No worries. You can still check out the base class and a subclass by scanning or clicking the QR code below, or by typing in the URL. More subclasses and unique familiars are available in other Loot Tavern publications.
{@link heliana.lt/Tamer|heliana.lt/Tamer}

entries.name: Bespoke Companions
entries: These crafted familiars are primed to be trained by monster tamers. A creature crafted in this way can be tamed by its bonded tamer without requiring an ability check. Upon being tamed, the creature can no longer be summoned by the find familiar spell and becomes a bespoke companion.

entries.name: Shared Resilience
entries: When a crafted familiar becomes a companion, it gains the 'Bonus Tamer Improvement' listed in its section without requiring the tamer to spend one of its improvements. Furthermore, as a tamer increases in power, its link to its bespoke companions emboldens them. When a character reaches 3rd, 5th, 11th, or 17th level in the tamer class, each of its bespoke companions gain 1 additional Hit Die. Each time this happens, the companion's hit point maximum increases by a roll of the Hit Die plus the companion's Constitution modifier (minimum of 0). These increases are also applied retroactively, should the tamer gain a bespoke companion after they have reached these levels.

entries.name: Monster Trainer
entries: As you gain levels in the tamer class, your companions become more powerful. Your companions use your proficiency bonus instead of their own, potentially increasing their attack, saving throw, and skill modifiers, and their saving throw DCs.

entries.name: Training
entries: Whenever you gain an Ability Score Improvement from gaining a level in the tamer class, you pass on your hardened resolve to all your companions, causing them to gain 1 additional Hit Die. Increase your companion's hit point maximum by rolling this Hit Die and adding your companion's Constitution modifier (minimum of 0).

Whenever you gain a level beyond 1st in this class, each companion to which you are bonded gains one improvement. Any newly gained companions receive these additional Hit Dice and improvements as if you had them since your 1st level in tamer. Each time your companion gains an improvement, choose from the Companion Improvement Training table or from its bespoke improvements.

entries.caption: Companion Improvement Training Table
rows: Increase one existing speed by 15 feet up to a maximum of 150% of the creature's base speed, rounded up to the nearest 5-foot increment*.

Your companion gains an additional Hit Die, increasing its hit point maximum. Increase your companion's hit point maximum by rolling this Hit Dice and adding your companion's Constitution modifier (minimum of 0).
Increase one of your companion's ability scores by 1, to a maximum of 20.
Your companion gains a +1 bonus to its attack and damage rolls made with its natural weapons or unarmed strikes**.
Your companion gains proficiency in one saving throw.
Your companion gains proficiency with one armour type or two weapons***.
footnotes: *E.g., a base speed of 30 feet can be increased to 45 feet maximum, a base speed of 25 feet can be increased to 40 feet, and a base speed of 20 feet to 30 feet.

** Your companions can each benefit from this feature once. When you reach 5th level in this class, your companions can each benefit from this feature twice. When you reach 9th level in this class, your companions can each benefit from this feature three times. This improvement does not affect attacks made with weapons.

*** Your companion can't gain proficiency in medium and heavy armour until it has gained proficiency in the prerequisite lighter armour types. For example, to gain proficiency in heavy armour, you must take this improvement 3 times: light, then medium, then heavy.

entries.name: Bespoke Improvements
entries: Companions that originated as bespoke familiars gains new ways to benefit from the improvements granted by a tamer's Monster Trainer feature. When a tamer gains a level, they can choose to grant these creatures an improvement from the creature- specific options listed in their familiar's monster trainer section instead of the normal ones available to all creatures tamed by a tamer.

entries.caption: Tamer Bespoke Companion Summary

colLabelRows.entry: Companion Gained on Training Bespoke Familiar

outro: For example, if a 10th level tamer trained a bespoke familiar, that creature would immediately gain 9 improvements and 6 Hit Dice.

data.name: Chapter 8: Hare Raising
entries: See {@adventure Hare Raising|LALoTT-HR}

data.name: Appendix A - Spells
entries: This appendix lists the new spells that can be found throughout the items and creatures of this book, as well as the classes that have access to those spells. Spells are arranged alphabetically in the Spells List table, accompanied by their level, school, and availability on class spell list.

entries.name: Biomancy
entries: Biomancy is a new school of magic introduced in this book. Biomantic spells revolve around living matter and its manipulation. They can transform skin, repair wounds, spread disease, and even stimulate cellular metabolism.

entries.name: Optional Rule: No Biomancy
entries: If you'd rather not use the school of biomancy in your world, you can elect to use the alternative school of magic supplied with each biomancy spell in this book. In the following spell lists, the superscript letter after the name indicates the alternate school that can be used if you do not wish to include this school in your world (E, evocation; H, Enchantment; N, Necromancy; T, transmutation).

data.name: Appendix B - Creatures
entries: This appendix introduces new creatures and swarms, particularly for the benefit of the Swarmslinger tamer. The creature categories are arranged alphabetically in this section. Within each category, creatures are arranged in limited alphabetical fashion or in CR order (as is the case with most creatures that have a swarm version).

entries.name: Legendary & Mythic Actions
entries: This book also features the bonemonger, a creature that makes use of optional legendary and mythic actions to improve the action economy of the boss fights. That creature is presented with several stat blocks for use with different adventurer levels, as well as lair actions usable in other scenarios.
